description:
You are an automation-workflow specialist who often receives raw CSV data and must quickly
    re-shape it for downstream systems that require simple tab-separated files (TSV).
    A colleague has placed the following source file in your workspace:

        /home/user/data/raw_contacts.csv

    raw_contacts.csv already exists and contains **exactly four comma-separated columns** in the
    order shown below (including the header line):

        id,name,email,department
        1,Alice Anderson,alice.anderson@example.com,Finance
        2,Bob Brown,bob.brown@example.com,Engineering
        3,Charlie Clark,charlie.clark@example.com,Marketing

    Your task is to perform a minimal “cut & paste” style column manipulation (use any commands
    you like, but 2–3 terminal commands are sufficient) and deliver the following two artefacts:

    1. A new directory called /home/user/report  
    2. Inside that directory, the two files listed below **with the exact byte-for-byte
       content/format that follows**.

    --------------------------------------------------------------------------
    a) /home/user/report/contact_report.tsv  
       • UTF-8 text, Unix line endings (\n)  
       • No header line  
       • Exactly three visible lines, each line ending with a single newline  
       • Two fields per line: the “name” column followed by the “email” column
         from the original CSV  
       • The fields must be separated by one horizontal tab character (U+0009)  
       • Final newline after the last line is required  

       Expected content (⟶ shows the tab character, **do not** include the arrow):

            Alice Anderson⟶alice.anderson@example.com
            Bob Brown⟶bob.brown@example.com
            Charlie Clark⟶charlie.clark@example.com

    b) /home/user/report/command_log.txt  
       • UTF-8 text, Unix line endings (\n)  
       • Exactly two lines, each one containing *only* the shell command you used
         (in the same order you executed them).  
       • The lines must not be commented, wrapped or prefixed—just raw commands.
         Example layout (do **not** copy this literally):

            first_command_here
            second_command_here

       The automated tests will not evaluate *which* commands you chose, only that
       there are precisely two non-empty lines.

    Notes & Constraints
    -------------------
    • Do not modify or delete the original /home/user/data/raw_contacts.csv file.  
    • You do **not** need to create intermediate files; if you do, they will be ignored
      by the tests.  
    • You have standard user privileges only.  
    • When you are done, verify the output with something like `cat -A` to ensure the
      tab characters and newlines are present exactly as described.

    Summary
    -------
    1. Extract the “name” (2nd) and “email” (3rd) columns from raw_contacts.csv.  
    2. Save them, tab-separated and header-less, to /home/user/report/contact_report.tsv.  
    3. Capture the two commands you actually ran in /home/user/report/command_log.txt.  

    A test harness will check:
       • The presence of /home/user/report/  
       • The precise byte-level content of contact_report.tsv  
       • That command_log.txt exists with exactly two non-blank lines.
truth:
<!--
        Ground-truth used by the automated checker.
        These values must remain private and unseen by the agent.
    -->

    <initial-setup>
        The file /home/user/data/raw_contacts.csv already exists with the exact
        contents shown in the public task description.
    </initial-setup>

    <expected>
        <file path="/home/user/report/contact_report.tsv">
Alice Anderson\talice.anderson@example.com\nBob Brown\tbob.brown@example.com\nCharlie Clark\tcharlie.clark@example.com\n
        </file>

        <!-- \t represents a single horizontal tab; \n a single LF newline -->

        <file path="/home/user/report/command_log.txt">
(Regex) ^[^\n]+\n[^\n]+\n?$
        </file>
    </expected>
